topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor was scrolling through a mundane feed of local real estate listings, half-listening to a podcast about gardening. Then, a thumbnail caught her eye: a picture of her childhood street, the familiar crooked oak tree at the corner, and the address she hadn't thought about in years. Her breath hitched. Her thumb hovered over the listing, then dropped onto the photo. A strange, cold tingling started in her stomach. She clicked through the details, her eyes darting back and forth across the photos, trying to reconcile the image with the reality of her own past. "No way," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet apartment. She reread the description, the words swimming before her eyes. A slow, deliberate shake ran through her shoulders. She closed the browser tab abruptly, her hand trembling slightly as she gripped the edge of the desk.

[story 2]

Mark was on his laptop, intending only to check the price of a new apartment downtown. The notification popped up, stark and undeniable: "14 Willow Lane – Available Now." He blinked, rereading the address twice. He leaned closer to the screen, his jaw tightening. The details that followed—the age of the house, the specific layout of the rooms—seemed to materialize with startling clarity. A sudden, sharp intake of air escaped his lungs. He pushed back from his chair, pacing the small living room. His movements became jerky, his gaze fixed on the screen as if expecting the information to vanish. "This can't be right," he muttered, his voice low and strained. He ran a hand through his hair, a gesture of profound disorientation.

[story 3]

For years, the address of the old bungalow had been a closed chapter, tucked away in dusty memory boxes. Liam was browsing a neighborhood forum, mindlessly clicking through updates, when a post demanding attention snagged his focus. It was a listing. His heart began to thump an uneven rhythm against his ribs. He scrolled down, his eyes widening incrementally with each new detail presented. A flush crept up his neck, warming his cheeks. He leaned back against the wall, suddenly feeling very unsteady. "That's... that's impossible," he breathed out, the words barely audible. He stared at the listing, his fingers flexing involuntarily against his phone screen, unable to pull his attention away from the unexpected reality unfolding on the display.
